tasks.description: Close the edge-orientation ambiguity class (FGAP-113): make edge orientation explicit metadata on every orientation-bearing relation_type and consume it symmetrically so a wrong-orientation guess is never silent on either side. WRITE — a backwards orientation-bearing edge is REJECTED at append (never auto-oriented; the reverted 2026-06-06 always-swap inverted canonically-authored edges, a08e586). READ — a walk/query issued against the wrong end of an orientation-bearing relation ERRORS (or normalizes with an explicit signal) rather than returning silent-empty. Covers ordering relations (task_depends_on_task and its same-kind siblings), the membership relations (reconciling the contains/includes vs positioned_in container-orientation split), and the unconstrained wildcard item_derived_from_item; reconciles relation_type names/metadata with the deriver contract; migrates any inverted edges. FGAP-007 (task_depends_on_task) is the write-ordering instance; the task_positioned_in_phase silent-empty is the read instance.
acceptance_criteria: Every orientation-bearing relation_type carries explicit endpoint-role/orientation metadata (extending the existing source_kinds/target_kinds) naming which stored endpoint holds which semantic role: the ordering relations (task_depends_on_task, story_depends_on_story, feature_depends_on_item, feature_gated_by_item, story_gated_by_item, requirement_depends_on_requirement, milestone_precedes_milestone, decision_supersedes_decision, research_supersedes_research), the membership relations (feature_contains_story, story_includes_item, task_positioned_in_phase, phase_positioned_in_milestone), and the wildcard item_derived_from_item — each either carries the metadata or is demonstrably exempt because it has no consumed orientation semantics; none is left with a name-vs-deriver or name-vs-container inversion.

WRITE enforcement: append-relation/append-relations REJECT a backwards orientation-bearing edge at write time with a message naming the expected orientation — a direction-correct edge is accepted, a backwards one is rejected, never silently stored and never auto-oriented. The rejection fires only for relation_types carrying the orientation metadata; relations without it are unaffected (no new false-positive rejections). Provable per relation_type: a name-faithful-but-backwards append is rejected; the canonical-direction append succeeds.

WRITE enforcement covers the wildcard: item_derived_from_item (source/target [*], no kind pin) is oriented by its declared role metadata, not by the absent kind check — a backwards item_derived_from_item append is rejected.

READ signaling: a walk-ancestors/walk-descendants (and any walk porcelain) issued against the WRONG end of an orientation-bearing relation ERRORS or returns an explicit wrong-orientation signal — not a bare empty array indistinguishable from a genuine absence. Provable: querying task_positioned_in_phase from the member end vs the container end yields distinguishable results (one the membership, the other an explicit wrong-direction signal), never two silently-different empties.

Membership orientation is reconciled to internal consistency: the container-orientation split (contains/includes store container=parent while positioned_in stores container=child) is resolved or the metadata explicitly encodes each so no learned heuristic is silently wrong for a sibling; documented on the relation_type surface.

Every existing edge in relations.json remains valid after the fix and any migration: none orphaned, dropped, or left inverted; any edge whose stored direction is backwards under the corrected contract is migrated to correct orientation; already-correct edges are unchanged.
The deriver's blocked/ready and lineage output is unchanged where edges were already correct and corrected only where an edge was inverted: no correctly-derived outcome changes.

context-validate (and context-validate-relations) passes against the active substrate after the fix and after any edge migration, with no new integrity findings; optionally a validate-time check flags any orientation-bearing edge whose stored direction violates its declared roles.

Tests assert both sides: a backwards ordering edge rejected + forwards accepted; the wildcard item_derived_from_item orientation enforced; the metadata-absent gate holds (no enforcement when orientation metadata is absent); a wrong-end read errors/signals rather than returning silent-empty; the deriver continues to treat {parent=prerequisite, child=dependent} correctly.
Build, check, and the full test suite pass at zero failures with the change in dist/.

tasks.description: Backfill decision derivation edges, then raise decision-shows-derivation to error. The decision-shows-derivation requires-edge invariant (the dedicated structural bite for the derive-decisions-from-facts convention) is live at severity WARNING: every decision must carry a decision_derived_from_item edge (the fact its resolution derives from) OR a decision_escalates_underdetermined edge (a genuinely-underdetermined escalated choice -> a framework-gap). 17 existing decisions currently warn. This task: (1) for each warning decision, read its reasoning and either edge decision_derived_from_item -> the substrate fact its resolution derived from (a research / convention / gap / prior-decision / rationale item; file a rationale item to reify a code/mandate fact that has no existing item), or decision_escalates_underdetermined -> the framework-gap capturing a genuinely-underdetermined choice; (2) once context-validate shows zero decision-shows-derivation warnings, raise the invariant severity from warning to error via amend-config. This completes the convention-articulation clean-after-backfill pattern for decision derivation, making a fork-as-decision (a decision with no shown derivation basis) UNFILEABLE.

tasks.description: Schema version-history audit (the `git log <file>` analog): a read-only command (e.g. `read-schema-history --schemaName <name>`) that prints the schema's history — which migrations ran, when, and from what base — plus the two recording fixes that make the history complete: stamp an applied-at time when a migration runs, and keep a pointer from each new baseline to the one it replaced. Without those two, the command could only show a partial story and "when" would still require digging through git commits: today the only timestamp on a migration decl is its authoring time, and each baseline advance overwrites the prior {content_hash, version} with no parent pointer (the replaced bodies sit unreferenced in the object store). The command composes what the substrate records: the registered migration decls for the schema (from→to, kind, author, authored-at, applied-at), the baseline lineage, and the installed/catalog versions.

tasks.description: Walk the migration chain incrementally (the `git bisect` analog): a read-only command (e.g. `walk-migration-chain --schemaName <name>`) that applies the schema's migration chain one step at a time in memory — apply migration 1, validate, apply migration 2, validate — to isolate which step breaks. Per step it reports the step's decl (from→to, kind) and which items' content changed; validation runs against the target schema on each cumulative prefix, attributing the first failure to the step whose application introduced it. Nothing is written — the walk is entirely in memory over the live block data and the resolved chain (the catalog chain, or the pinned pending-blocked chain when the schema is blocked). Limitation stated honestly: the catalog ships only the latest schema body per kind, so a step's output cannot be validated against an intermediate schema version (only against the final target, or an intermediate body that happens to be pinned in the object store); the op isolates the breaking transform step, which is the operator's ask.

tasks.description: Add a write-time edge kind/category validator. This hoists the existing post-hoc kind check (validateContext, context-sdk.ts:1987-2021, FGAP-086/DEC-0037) to the write surface (the raw appendRelationsForDir, context.ts:649, cannot resolve endpoint kinds under the documented layering at context.ts:617-625). Presence-gate on source_kinds/target_kinds exactly as the validate-time check (context-sdk.ts:1990) so currently-passing writes are unaffected. Resolves the write-time-enforcement half of FGAP-090. Distinct from TASK-027 (ordering-edge endpoint-role DIRECTION, ordering-only; this checks source/target KIND + category across all relation_types).
acceptance_criteria: A real append-relation CLI invocation writing an edge whose parent or child item kind is not in the relation_type's source_kinds/target_kinds is rejected at write with an error naming the offending endpoint kind + relation_type, and the edge is not persisted.

A relation_type with neither source_kinds nor target_kinds set is not rejected at write (the presence-gate at context-sdk.ts:1990 is preserved at the write surface).

Write-time and validate-time kind semantics agree: an edge the write rejects, validateContext (context-sdk.ts:1987-2021) would also flag; an edge the write accepts, validateContext does not flag for kind. The existing validateContext check is unchanged.
npm run build + check + full test pass with 0 failures; a fresh adversarial probe confirms no over-reject of presence-gated (unset-kinds) types and write/validate parity.

tasks.description: Extend `currentState` (packages/pi-context/src/context-sdk.ts:682-825) — the derivation behind context-current-state / `/context status` — so task readiness honors `task_gated_by_item` gates, not only `task_depends_on_task`. A planned task carrying a `task_gated_by_item` edge to a target NOT in the `complete` bucket must be reported blocked (target id in `blockedBy`) and excluded from `nextActions`; a gate target reaching the `complete` bucket releases the gate. Gate satisfaction reuses `bucket(target) === "complete"` via resolveStatusVocabulary (status-vocab.ts) — kind-general, the same mechanism the status-consistency invariant engine uses (context-sdk.ts:2186-2227); NOT a net-new completeness check. Both consumption sites fold gate parents: `blocked` (incompleteDeps→depParentsOf, :727-730/:742-751) and the `nextActions` topo predecessor function (:790). Scope is strictly the literal relation_type `task_gated_by_item`; `decision_gated_by_item` and non-task `*_gated_by_item` are inert (currentState buckets only tasks) and OUT of scope — the config-driven generalization is FEAT-004. NOW slice of FGAP-061.
acceptance_criteria: A planned (non-`complete`-bucket) task with a `task_gated_by_item` edge to a target NOT in the `complete` bucket is reported in `currentState().blocked` with that target id present in its `blockedBy`, and is absent from `nextActions`.

When the gate target reaches the `complete` bucket (gap→`closed`, decision→`enacted`, feature→`complete`, task→`completed`), the same task is reported ready (in `nextActions`, absent from `blocked`). Gate satisfaction is implemented as `bucket(target) === "complete"` via resolveStatusVocabulary — no kind-special-casing.

A task with BOTH `task_depends_on_task` and `task_gated_by_item`: `blockedBy` is the UNION of unsatisfied dep-parents and unsatisfied gate-targets; with no gate edges present, `currentState` output is byte-identical to current behavior (no regression to dependency derivation).
`nextActions` topo ordering excludes gate-blocked tasks — the topo predecessor function folds gate parents alongside dep parents (:790), so a gate-blocked task is never emitted as a next action.
A dangling `task_gated_by_item` target (id resolves to no item) is treated as satisfied (non-blocking), mirroring the dangling-dep guard (:732) — no throw, no block.
Scope confinement: `decision_gated_by_item` and any non-`task_gated_by_item` edge do NOT alter `currentState` output; the gate filter matches the literal `task_gated_by_item`.

A gate target in a terminal-abandoned status (`wontfix`/`superseded`/`cancelled`) keeps the gated task blocked (per criterion 2's `=== "complete"` rule); the `currentState` JSDoc (:674-680) documents this as the FEAT-004 refinement boundary.

New tests in `context-sdk.test.ts` (the `currentState` describe, :2604-2745, cloning the `task_depends_on_task` template at :2633-2678) cover: gate-blocked, gate-released-on-completion, cross-kind gate targets, dep+gate union, dangling gate target, terminal-abandoned gate target.

Gates green with full output (no pipe-to-tail): `npm run build`, `npm run check`, `npm test`. Runtime demo: real `context-current-state` (CLI) against a /tmp substrate showing a gated task as blocked, then ready after the gate target is completed — not a mocked assertion. Fresh-context adversarial probe on the gate logic (over-block, under-block, kind-leak into non-task gates, topo/`blocked` parity).

docs-surface-sync: extend the `currentState` JSDoc (:674-680) and the `context-current-state` op `description`/`promptSnippet` (ops-registry.ts:1154-1167) to state gate-aware readiness (usage-only, what it does); regen SKILL.md if the op string changed; add a CHANGELOG `[Unreleased]` entry.

`blockersOf` de-duplicates the dep+gate union, preserving discovery order (deps before gates): a target appearing in both the unsatisfied-dependency and unsatisfied-gate sets, or a redundant exact-duplicate `task_depends_on_task` edge, is listed once in `blockedBy`. Exact-duplicate edges are not producible through the write API (`appendRelations` skips an existing `(parent, child, relation_type)` triple, context.ts:649-663), so on every API-produced substrate this dedup is invisible and the single-edge no-gate path stays byte-identical to the prior derivation; on a hand-duplicated-edge substrate it collapses the redundant blocker to a single entry — a strict improvement, not a dependency-derivation regression.

tasks.description: Make the operator pi-context binary a publish-free local copy of the working tree, immune to dev builds. Today /opt/homebrew/bin/pi-context is an npm link symlink into packages/pi-context-cli/dist/, so npm run build (rm -rf dist && tsc) repoints and transiently removes the live operator binary. Retire the link; install the operator binary as a packed copy of the working-tree CLI + its @davidorex dependency set (current code, not the stale registry 0.31.0); add a promote:cli root npm-task fronting scripts/promote-cli.mjs (build, pack the set, install into the global prefix, verify a non-symlink copy), fitting the version:* / release:* composer convention. Scope: the binary coupling only — not the proving-ground harness, a CI gate, or schema-evolution work.
acceptance_criteria: realpath of /opt/homebrew/bin/pi-context resolves under /opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/@davidorex/pi-context-cli/, not under /Users/david/Projects/workflowsPiExtension.
npm ls -g --link lists no @davidorex/pi-context-cli link to the repo.
The installed operator reflects working-tree code, not the registry 0.31.0 release.
A repo npm run build leaves the realpath target of /opt/homebrew/bin/pi-context unchanged in inode and content hash.
After a repo npm run build, pi-context --version exits 0.
With packages/pi-context-cli/dist deleted, pi-context --help exits 0.
A re-promote after a repo source edit changes the content hash of the realpath target of /opt/homebrew/bin/pi-context.
No file under the repo instructs npm link for the operator binary.
packages/pi-context-cli/README.md and the root README.md document the copy install of the operator.

The real-global prefix is resolved non-poisonably: an inherited npm_config_prefix (which `npm run promote:cli`, the documented invocation, always injects as the true global) does NOT divert the resolved or installed target — the `npm prefix -g` probe and the `npm i -g` install both run under an env with every npm_config_* scrubbed, so the resolved prefix reflects the true global regardless of any inherited value. The inherited prefix is NOT refused: refusing it would break the tool's own documented path and provide no protection the scrub does not, since npm's legitimate injection and a hostile override are indistinguishable at the env-var level.
Every global install or removal the promote performs acts only on the validated target prefix.
A target prefix whose realpath resolves to or under the repo root is refused — no global install or removal occurs.
A failed global install leaves the prior operator runnable and its installed payload unchanged.
Invoking the promote with the argument token --prefix=<dir> installs the operator at <dir>/bin/pi-context.
tasks.notes: Addresses issue-004. Scope: the binary coupling only.

tasks.description: Add a load-time config-migration path to loadConfigForDir (context.ts:565-582): read the config's schema_version and, on mismatch with the bundled config schema version, walk the schema-name-keyed migration chain for config before validating — mirroring validateBlockWithMigrationForDir (schema-validator.ts:205-242), including its fail-fast throw when no chain resolves. Add a config migration registry entry and the declarative-transform primitive (map_each) that maps array elements within a migration step.
acceptance_criteria: loadConfigForDir reads the config's schema_version, compares it to the bundled config schema version, and on mismatch consults the schema-name-keyed migration chain for `config` (via the project MigrationRegistry built from migrations.json) and runs it forward before validating; on mismatch with no resolvable chain it fails fast (throws, mirroring validateBlockWithMigrationForDir's version-mismatch throw), never falling back to raw validation; on version match it does not consult the registry.

A `config` migration declaration covering the on-disk config's schema_version up to the bundled schema version is filed in the substrate's migrations.json via write-schema-migration, such that loadConfigForDir resolves a NON-EMPTY chain for it; the registry is not empty for `config` at the demonstrated version gap.

A config whose schema_version lags the bundled config schema by the real on-disk gap (1.0.0 → current) loads valid through the migrated path, carried forward in memory, with zero config-load failure of any exit class — demonstrated by runtime reproduction on a scratch substrate, not by a version-matched no-op.

The map_each declarative-transform primitive is added to the TransformOp union (migrations-store.ts:64-68), the migrations.schema.json TransformOp oneOf, and applyOp (migration-registry-loader.ts), with two declared modes: table mode (`path` + `table` [+ `fallback`, enum parent|child, defaulting to "parent" when absent]) replaces each string element of the array at `path` by table[element], or by {relation_type: element, item_endpoint: fallback} when unmatched, with non-string elements passing through unchanged; and set-on-each mode (`path` + `field` + `value`) sets element[field]=value on each object element of the array at `path`. A migration step exercising each mode transforms the array elements at `path` accordingly, verified against a fixture.
A current-version config (schema_version equal to the bundled schema version) loads byte-unchanged: no migration applied, registry not consulted.
TASK-070 is recorded as the config instance of FEAT-010; its scope is the config instance, not the block-data class.

samples/migrations.json ships an identity `config` migration declaration covering 1.0.0 → the bundled config schema version; a fresh init → accept-all → install substrate carries that declaration in its migrations.json, so loadConfigForDir resolves a NON-EMPTY chain for `config` and a catalog-born substrate loads through the migration path, never reaching the no-chain throw.

tasks.description: Add a sanctioned config-repair path that applies the corrective mutation and validates the result, rather than loading-and-validating the stored config before the repair, so an already-invalid config is recoverable. Scope: any invalid config regardless of version, distinct from the version-gap migration. The repair mechanism is a `force`/`repair` opt on amend-config that bypasses the pre-load validation and validates the result via the existing write path (block-api.ts:892); derived from the existing op surface, no DEC.
acceptance_criteria: An invalid config, including schema_version equal to current with an invalid body, is restored to validity through a sanctioned CLI op without a forbidden direct edit of config.json.
The repair validates the corrected result, not the pre-existing stored config; it does not throw on load before the mutation applies.
Both the version-gap subset and the no-version-delta invalidity are recoverable.

The repair is a `force`/`repair` mode of the existing amend-config op — the same config write, mirroring its existing dryRun opt (context.ts:1229): it skips the pre-load validation (context.ts:1251) and relies on amend-config's existing pre-write result-validation (block-api.ts:892-895). It is an opt on amend-config, not a separate recover op: config-repair is a validation-skip mode of an existing write, not a missing library capability, so the opt is derived from the existing amend-config surface.
tasks.notes: Repair mechanism is DERIVED from the existing op surface, not deferred to a DECISION. tasks.title: Bash chokepoint hook: destructive-git / --no-verify sentinel, gate-command pipe-masking, release/push hold (FEAT-013 pillar 1 + 4)
tasks.description:
PreToolUse(Bash) chokepoint, segment-split on ;&| so chained commands are caught. Three blocks:
- Destructive git (the commit-gate evasion class — --no-verify, -n (its short form), a HUSKY= env prefix, -c core.hooksPath= — plus reset --hard, rebase, push --force, tag -d, unmerged branch-delete) — blocked without a per-invocation user sentinel.
- Gate-command masking (npm test / run check / run build piped or truncated) — blocked.
- HELD actions (npm run release:*, npm publish, non-durably-authorized git push) — blocked without a user-minted grant object.
The grant object is itself write-shielded: direct Edit/Write/NotebookEdit and Bash write-forms targeting it are denied except via the granting flow (the TASK-094 pattern applied to the harness's files); paired detection: a periodic cross-check that each grant open corresponds to a user message (claude-history) — natural home the audit-substrate-currency skill or a sibling.
Escapes are user-attributed per the provenance-ceremony shape (same-turn re-issue forbidden). FEAT-013 R2, R3, R4.

npm test|run check|run build piped or tailed is blocked; the bare command passes; a set -o pipefail form is permitted.
npm run release:*, npm publish, and non-authorized git push are blocked without a user-minted grant object; a covered grant passes.
On a sentinel-required refusal this guard sets the TASK-096 guard-fired marker (lifecycle per TASK-096).
The grant object is write-shielded (deniable paths only via the granting flow) and grant opens are cross-checkable against user messages.
Regression cells for each true-positive block, each legitimate pass, and the marker set-on-refusal.

tasks.title: Phase-state layer: per-work-unit pipeline state, SessionStart seed/reconcile, event-minted transitions (FEAT-013 pillar 2 — the keystone)
tasks.description: A machine-readable per-work-unit pipeline state (.claude/state/pipeline.json or a substrate-native work-order): {taskId, state (SURFACED..CLOSED), delegation (orchestrator | subagent-session role marker distinguishing who owns the current edit), branch, planStamp, guardFiredMarker, demoEvidence, probeVerdict, findings[]}. Two field classes. Coarse position (state, task status, verification edges, branch) is SessionStart-reconciled from substrate truth (context-status), never from narrative. Session-local fields (planStamp, delegation, guardFiredMarker, demoEvidence, probeVerdict, findings[]) are not substrate-native (requirements report R14, in R-0028's findings doc, defers that) and persist in the phase-state file across sessions, giving TASK-099's closure gates a stable source. Transitions minted only by hook-verified events (PostToolUse(ExitPlanMode) -> PLANNED via the plan-archive pattern; feature-branch commit -> IMPLEMENTED; complete-task -> CLOSED). The delegation field is SET on Agent-dispatch of a coding subagent and CLEARED on its return (PostToolUse(Agent) — one of the unverified hook events; verify-first). Guard-fired marker: set by any sentinel-bearing guard on a sentinel-required refusal, cleared by TASK-098's Stop hook at turn boundary; blocks same-turn sentinel re-issue. The phase-state file is itself write-shielded: direct Edit/Write/NotebookEdit and Bash write-forms targeting .claude/state/* are denied except via the minting hooks/driver (the TASK-094 pattern applied to the harness's files). The state hooks consult so they check 'is this tool call legal in this state' instead of re-deriving process. Keystone for the source-edit gate, the ask gate, and the findings ledger. FEAT-013 R11.
acceptance_criteria: A SessionStart hook seeds/reconciles the phase-state file: the coarse pipeline position (state, branch, task status) from substrate truth (context-status), never from any narrative cache.
A mid-pipeline session restart preserves the session-local fields (planStamp, delegation, guardFiredMarker, demoEvidence, probeVerdict, findings[]), so TASK-099's closure gates keep a stable source.

State transitions are minted by hook-verified events (ExitPlanMode, feature-branch commit, complete-task), each demonstrated, and the delegation field is set on coding-subagent dispatch and cleared on return.
Direct Edit/Write/NotebookEdit and Bash write-forms targeting .claude/state/* are denied except via the minting hooks/driver.
The state file is machine-readable and queryable cheaply by the downstream deny hooks.

tasks.title: Stop-hook ask gate: three-stops filter + guard-fired marker lifecycle (FEAT-013 pillar 3 — the chaos-driver foreclosure)
tasks.description: A Stop hook (the only mechanical point that sees a turn's final message; currently unused) that blocks turn-end on an ask failing the three-legitimate-stops filter. Every other ask-shaped closer ("on your go", "now or later", "shall I", "want me to") is relitigation — blocked, governing rule named. Regex first; invokeMonitor classification if regex is too blunt. tasks.title: Session driver: walk a work unit through the state machine as the path of least resistance (FEAT-013 pillar 2 — the predictable half)
tasks.description: A driver skill/command (precedent: .claude/skills/run-pi-project-workflows/driver.mjs; the workflow executor's control-plane/subordinate-subprocess architecture) that walks a work unit through the canonical state machine: prompts the current state's required action, dispatches the right agent kind per state (Explore for EXPLORED, general-purpose for IMPLEMENTED, fresh-context for PROBED), runs the npm gates itself, mints phase-state transitions, and refuses out-of-order actions. The driver composes each dispatch prompt deterministically (WASC renderer pattern), inlining the project mandates and the role contracts (stop-on-ambiguity for implementing agents; the machine-parsed output contract for probe/audit agents), since subagents do not receive the UserPromptSubmit injection. Autonomous re-dispatch loops carry a hard iteration cap (then STOP and escalate per mandate-008) and terminate only on deterministic signals (gate exit codes; the parsed findings count). With the phase-state layer it makes the state machine the interface rather than a policy the model recalls — the 'predictable' half of the directive (hooks make deviation fail; the driver makes compliance the path of least resistance). Depends on the full deny surface + state layer. FEAT-013 R17.

tasks.title: Scheduled currency-audit loop — transpose the WASC detection half (FEAT-013 detection pillar)
tasks.description: Transpose the WASC scheduled currency auditor into this project: (1) .claude/agents/context-currency-auditor.md — read-only forensic auditor; reads (never writes) tmp/context-audits/.watermark.json; missing/invalid watermark forces FULL audit, never treated as clean; fast path when baseline == HEAD with clean tree; incremental audit scoped to git log <baseline>..HEAD otherwise; check engine runs the mechanical core first (npx tsx scripts/check-context-currency.ts exit-code-parsed; pi-context context-reconcile --dryRun; pi-context context-validate) then the judgment sweep scoped to the window (bucket-lag, fired free-text stale_conditions, closure omissions — the TASK-094 class, decision-before-implementation criteria) via pi-context ops; claude-history CLI attribution per finding; impression-only findings forbidden; direct-drive discipline stated in the agent body. (2) .claude/commands/audit-context-currency.md — loop-friendly non-interactive command: dispatch auditor, persist report to tmp/context-audits/audit-<TS>.md, PushNotification iff non-clean (once, <200 chars, leads with action + report path; clean = silent), write watermark with last_clean_head = HEAD iff verdict CLEAN with findings 0 and no unverified checks, otherwise carry the prior last_clean_head forward UNCHANGED (advance-only-on-clean); non-clean report names /audit-substrate-currency as the enactment path. (3) tmp/context-audits/ watermark home (gitignored). (4) /loop 10m /audit-context-currency scheduling convention documented in CLAUDE.md. tasks.title: Enumerate substrate-ID citations inside code comments (issue-012 tooling)
tasks.description: Write scripts/scan-comment-citations.ts: a standalone, informational (non-gate) script that walks every packages/*/src TypeScript file, uses the TS compiler API scanner (ts.createScanner with skipTrivia=false) to visit every comment token (JSDoc /** */, block /* */, line //) exactly once, and matches a citation regex against each comment's text to enumerate every substrate-tracker-ID-shaped token (FGAP-\d{3}, DEC-\d{4}, FEAT-\d{3}, TASK-\d{3}, VER-\d{3}, PHASE-[A-Z0-9-]+, MILE-\d{3,}, REVIEW-\d+, RAT-\d+, CTX-\d+, WO-\d+, STORY-\d+, PLAN-\d+, REQ-\d+, R-\d{4}, ISSUE-\d+/issue-\d+, JI-\d{3}) found in comments. Outputs JSON to stdout: scannedPackages, totalInstances, totalFiles, byId, byFile, and a full instances[] array (file, line, id, commentKind, snippet). This is the enumeration step needed before issue-012 (frozen-era ID citations in pi-agent-dispatch resolving to unrelated live substrate items) and the general ID-rot problem class can be resolved -- it establishes exactly how many such citations exist and where. Corrects two confirmed gaps in the existing citation-rot-scanner.ts pattern (which this script cannot import -- CITATION_RE is unexported and pi-context's package.json exports map has no subpath for that file): PHASE ids are alphanumeric slugs live today (PHASE-PORT-OPS etc.), not the numeric-only PHASE-\d{3,} that scanner uses; MILE-\d{3,} has no alternative at all in that scanner despite 9 live MILE-00N items. Scope discipline: enumeration and counting only -- this script does NOT resolve each cited ID against the live substrate to judge staleness; that is a distinct follow-on capability, out of scope here. Investigation basis: analysis produced during this session's issue-012 discussion (citation-rot-scanner.ts read in full; phase.schema.json / milestone.schema.json id patterns confirmed against live .context data).
acceptance_criteria: npx tsx scripts/scan-comment-citations.ts runs clean over the full monorepo (no crash) and produces valid JSON.

Runtime demo: the output's instances[] array contains FEAT-006, TASK-088, TASK-091, DEC-0044, DEC-0047, DEC-0018, DEC-0014 entries at the file/line locations issue-012 already cites in packages/pi-agent-dispatch/src/work-order-loop.ts and run-work-order-loop-tool.ts -- this is the load-bearing correctness check, not just 'it ran'.

scripts/scan-comment-citations.test.ts exists with unit cells against synthetic fixtures (JSDoc with 2 citations, line comment with 1, zero-citation file) asserting exact instances/byId/totalInstances shape, plus a live-repo integration cell pinning the issue-012 FEAT-006/TASK-088/TASK-091 finding as a regression.
npm run build + npm run check + npm test exit 0 (the new test file runs via the existing scripts/*.test.ts glob in the test:migration script -- no new wiring required).

A fresh adversarial probe independently confirms the corrected PHASE-[A-Z0-9-]+ and MILE-\d{3,} patterns actually match real live IDs of those families (or confirms zero such citations exist in comments today, not that the pattern silently can't match them) -- scoped strictly to enumeration accuracy, not broader.
The script is not wired into npm run check, husky, or CI -- it is informational tooling only; making it a gate is an explicit future decision, not assumed here.

tasks.title: Scope file-changed stale_conditions to the cited line range instead of whole-file hash
tasks.description: Add an optional lines field to the file-changed stale_conditions schema branch (research.schema.json, additive, 1.1.0 -> 1.2.0, identity migration -- no data transform needed). Add a new computeFileLineRangeHash helper (packages/pi-context/src/content-hash.ts) that parses a comma-separated lines spec (e.g. '483-492,597-614,651-652'), extracts and hashes only those lines' content. Wire it into block-api.ts's stampDeclaredBaselines and context-sdk.ts's evaluateStalenessCandidates: when a file-changed condition carries lines, use the line-scoped hash; when absent, keep the existing whole-file hash unchanged. State the residual limitation explicitly in code comments and at closure: this is line-number-based, not content-anchored, so an unrelated edit earlier in the file that shifts line numbers can still mis-fire -- a smaller-scope version of the prior imprecision, not a total fix. Also correct R-0030's own live-fired stale_conditions entries (the triggering instance): add lines from its sibling citations[] entries where available (5 of its 7 file-changed entries have a matching citations[] lines value) and re-stamp baseline_hash under the new algorithm via the sanctioned write path (update-block-item), never a direct edit. Fixes FGAP-137.
acceptance_criteria: research.schema.json's file-changed branch declares an optional lines field (type string, no pattern, matching the existing citations[].lines/evidence[].lines convention); schema version bumped 1.1.0 -> 1.2.0 with an identity migration registered.

stampDeclaredBaselines and evaluateStalenessCandidates both use the line-scoped hash when lines is present on a file-changed condition, and the unchanged whole-file hash when it is absent.

Runtime demo (load-bearing, not just R-0030 looking clean): a fresh file-changed condition with lines set, an edit OUTSIDE that range does NOT fire the condition, an edit INSIDE that range DOES fire it.

Implement: the install ceremony (context-install / accept-all) materializes real agent spec files into <contextDir>/agents/ from a config-declared installed_agents[] list, mirroring the existing installed_schemas[]/installed_blocks[] materialization mechanism -- not merely pointing at the read-only bundled directory FGAP-127's shipped fix resolves from. Materialized files are ordinary project files: editable, deletable, and customizable by the project that installs them. The existing bundled-builtinDir tier (FGAP-127) is unaffected and remains the fallback when no local copy exists, per the loader's existing project > user > bundled precedence.
acceptance_criteria: context-install (and accept-all) materializes real agent spec files into <contextDir>/agents/ from a config-declared installed_agents[] list -- mirroring installed_schemas[]/installed_blocks[] -- verified by runtime demo on a fresh substrate.

Regression tests cover: fresh-install materialization, precedence (local copy beats bundled), and edit-then-redispatch behavior change.

tasks.description: User directive (verbatim, this session): 'first we need a script that will use nlp processing to pre-identify candidates needing full agent treatment for validating / de-hedging / correcting, for all substrate items, not just gaps.' Implemented as scripts/scan-substrate-hedges.ts (read-only, informational, non-gate; matching this repo's scan-comment-citations.ts precedent): scans EVERY block kind declared in the active substrate's config (config.block_kinds via loadConfig -- no hardcoded block list, per this project's derive-from-registry convention) and every schema-declared prose field of every item (a string property counts as prose iff its schema declares no enum/pattern/format/const -- ids, statuses, dates, hashes all declare one; the walk recurses through arrays-of-strings and arrays-of-objects; x-prompt-budget presence is recorded on findings but not required -- no hardcoded field list). All substrate access flows through the canonical pi-context surface (loadConfig / readSchema / readBlock), never raw fs reads of the substrate's *.json. Heuristic pattern matching in three weighted categories, grounded in the hedges the manual FGAP-124/125/126/127 provenance audits actually confirmed: (1) fork -- enumerated-alternative language (and/or; either...or; dash-or as in FGAP-125's live '-- or amend the schema'; semicolon-or; paren-or and or+gerund as in pre-correction FGAP-126; lettered '(a)...(b)' option lists -- numbered '(1)...(2)' deliberately unmatched because live substrate prose uses it to enumerate facts, not alternatives; 'alternatively'; option-enumeration cues); (2) deferral -- explicit decision-postponement ('as a decision' -- the exact FGAP-126 augmentation phrase -- TBD, to be determined/decided, not yet decided, open question, user's / user scope call, up to the user, undecided/undetermined, deferred, left/remains open, pending/needs a decision, decide later, punt, weak 'not yet'); (3) modal-hedge -- low-weight uncertainty vocabulary (might, could, may need/want/require, possibly/perhaps, probably/presumably, eventually, at some point, unclear/uncertain, unknown, later, seems/appears to, if needed). Every match carries pattern id + category + weight (1-4); an item's score is the summed weight across its fields, candidates rank by score with a min-score threshold (default 2, --min-score). Output: a single machine-readable JSON report (tool / generated_at / minScore / per-block summaries / reconciled totals / candidates sorted by score; per match: field path, matched text, surrounding-context snippet) written to a timestamped path under gitignored tmp/substrate-hedge-scans/ by default; --output overrides the path, --stdout prints the full report instead. The ranked candidate list is what an orchestrator works through with the gap-fork-provenance-audit-brief-template (analysis/2026-07-11-gap-fork-provenance-audit-brief-template.md) or its generalization to other block kinds. Weights rank, they do not judge: grounded forks (FGAP-124's and FGAP-127's were audited and left standing) are still flagged by design -- grounded-vs-invented is exactly what the downstream per-item agent audit determines; recall is deliberately favored over precision. The script itself corrects nothing and makes zero substrate writes -- pre-identification only.
acceptance_criteria: Script discovers block kinds and their schemas dynamically from the active substrate's config (block_kinds, schema field definitions) -- no hardcoded list of block names or field names anywhere in the script.
Scans every schema-declared prose field (string properties without enum/pattern/format/const, including strings nested inside arrays and objects) across every item in every discovered block.

Flags matches for all three marker categories (enumerated-option forks, explicit-deferral phrases, vague/modal hedging), each match carrying pattern id, category, weight, matched text, and a surrounding-context snippet; candidates are ranked by summed match weight with a configurable min-score threshold.

Output is a single machine-readable JSON file at a timestamped path (default under gitignored tmp/substrate-hedge-scans/; --output and --stdout available); the script makes zero substrate writes (read-only).

Running it against the current substrate's framework-gaps block produces a report that includes FGAP-125's still-unaudited proposed_resolution or-clause as a known true-positive sanity check (met at implementation: FGAP-125 flagged on proposed_resolution via the dash-or fork pattern; the test suite pins this conditionally so the cell passes vacuously once the fork is audited away downstream).

Unit tests cover each heuristic marker category against synthetic true-positive and true-negative fixtures, the schema-driven prose-field classification/extraction, per-item aggregation, and live-substrate structural invariants (tsx --test, matching this project's testing convention).
Full build/check/test clean.

tasks.description: Implement FGAP-043's decided canonical resolution: add an x-rhetorical-criteria schema extension keyword to every block-item schema (18 kinds, catalog + active reconciled via /context update), plus a new write-time enforcement function wired into writeTypedFile's single write funnel in block-api.ts. Enforcement is diff-scoped to only the item(s) actually changed in a given write (never the whole file) to avoid bricking future writes on pre-existing violations. Two mechanically-checkable rhetorical-register rules are enforced: word-count caps via the existing-but-dormant x-prompt-budget.words, and no-provenance/git/prior-state-narration via a shared DEFAULT_PROHIBITED_PATTERNS regex set. The other four rhetorical-register rules remain human-review-enforced, matching current enforcement level. Includes backfilling session-notes.schema.json's active copy which is missing $id/version/title/description entirely (a pre-existing gap discovered during this task's own investigation, fixed inline as a one-line prerequisite, not separately filed). Plan: /Users/david/.claude/plans/modular-herding-widget.md.

tasks.description: Implement FGAP-142: export citation-rot-scanner.ts's CITATION_RE (or a small detection-core function) via a package subpath so scan-comment-citations.ts and any future consumer reuse it instead of hand-duplicating the regex. Extend the scanner to also detect tracker-ID citations inside JSDoc/line comments in source files, delta-scoped: only a citation introduced in the current diff/working-tree change is flagged (via a git-diff-aware check against the base branch or staged changes), never a pre-existing one -- so the gate can run today without retroactively blocking on the ~500+ instances FGAP-136's rollout already cleaned up.
